field so host apps can

Kept here (not in

) so every runtime error ships as

Stable failure code for config errors. See

runtime error.

Never thrown directly — subclass instead. The abstract

field
forces every concrete subclass to declare a stable, machine-readable
identifier, which host apps use to branch on specific failure modes
without string-matching on the message.

The

works correctly across the
prototype chain even when the runtime is transpiled to older ES
targets or consumed via CJS interop. With

it is
a harmless no-op — kept so the code remains portable.

Stable, machine-readable failure identifier.

Subclasses must override this with a constant literal so host
apps can branch on

without string-matching on the
human-readable message.
Thrown when a registered export format's

implementation
fails.

Raised by the export pipeline (

) when a format throws or
returns a rejected promise. The

field identifies which
format failed so host apps can surface a targeted message
("HTML export failed: …") without introspecting the cause.

Advisory diagnostics should flow through

instead — this error is reserved for hard failures where no
content could be produced at all.

Also raised by

is the only hook whose
errors abort the publish and propagate to the host.

The

field pinpoints the offending plugin so host apps
can surface a precise error message even if several plugins are
loaded.

of the plugin that caused the failure.

For errors that involve two plugins (e.g. a duplicate export
format id) this field holds the second plugin's id, and both
ids appear in the error message.

Description of a single component in a shape an LLM can reason
about — name, purpose, props, and an optional concrete example.

An array of these is passed to the LLM so it knows what components
are available and how to use them. Produced by
; Core only owns the destination shape.
The component name as registered in the Puck config (e.g.

Human-readable description of what the component does and when
to use it. Forwarded verbatim into the LLM prompt — write it
as guidance to a collaborator.

Optional concrete example of a valid prop bag. The LLM uses
this as a one-shot example of how the fields should look when
populated; leaving it off falls back to zero-shot from the
field descriptions alone.

Description of a single prop on a component, in a shape an LLM can
reason about.

(Phase 3) produces an
array of these from a Puck

field descriptor. The AI
copilot plugin then feeds them into its system prompt so the model
knows which props it can set and what shape each one takes.

Recursive:

Human-readable description of what the prop is for. Forwarded
verbatim into the LLM prompt, so write it in the voice of
instructions to a collaborator ("The headline shown at the
top of the hero section").
fields, the schema of a single item. For

fields, a pseudo-schema describing one key of
the object (caller may provide several by nesting).

Absent on scalar field types.

). Must match the key in the component's

fields, the allowed values. Each option
carries both the machine-readable

(written into the

(shown to the LLM so it
picks the right one).

Whether the prop must be set for the component to render
correctly. Defaults to

when omitted — the LLM will
treat the field as optional.

The full input to a single LLM generation call.

Constructed by the AI copilot plugin at the moment the user asks
for generation.

once per session and cached; the remaining fields

Every component the LLM is allowed to emit, described as an

. The LLM cannot use a component that

Optional snapshot of the current Puck page data. Lets the LLM
generate contextual edits (e.g. "add a CTA to the existing
hero") instead of always producing a full page from scratch.

) so
the LLM generates copy in the right language.

. The entry is
intentionally minimal — prompt text and a timestamp, no result
payload, no error record. Failed runs are recorded the same way
as successful ones because the UI typically wants to re-run or
tweak a prompt regardless of how it ended.
The prompt the user submitted, verbatim.

Full shape of the AI store — read fields plus the three write

The user's prompt history, oldest first. New prompts are

trims
to the last 10 entries when persisting, and

while a generation is in flight. **Never persisted**
— a reload always lands on

so the UI can never be
stuck in a spinner.
Error message from the most recent failed call, or

when the most recent call succeeded. **Never persisted** —
showing a stale error after a reload is worse than showing
nothing.

The prompt currently (or most recently) being generated
against, or
before any call has been made. Ephemeral.

Empty the in-memory history array. Persisted storage is
overwritten on the next

— there is no explicit
storage-clear call because the next

run will
write an empty array.

string
to record a failure; pass

in the

with the current timestamp.

A single issue produced by validating an LLM's response against
the expected component/field shape.

(Phase 3) owns the runtime check that emits
these; Core just owns the shape so validator output flows through
a stable contract.

Human-readable message describing what went wrong. Suitable
for display in a dev console or a toast.

JSON-pointer-style path to the offending value inside the LLM
response (e.g.

). Empty string means
the issue applies to the response as a whole.

— the response is usable but suspect (e.g. an

-

— the response is unusable (e.g. required field

only when the response contains zero
issues with

issues. A valid response may still carry

A nested options object (instead of a second positional arg for
the env bag directly) leaves room for future knobs —
deprecation handling, plugin discovery overrides, strict mode
toggles — without breaking the public signature.

Environment variable bag to consume for Layer 2.

when that global is available. Tests
and non-Node runtimes can inject a synthetic bag so the factory
stays deterministic and never touches the real process env.

is performed exactly once by the export pipeline before any format
runs, ensuring every exporter sees the same input regardless of
how the page was authored.

argument as deeply read-only.

exporters in the same pipeline.

. Host apps may surface these in a

Full shape of the export store. Split into read fields (the state
snapshot) and setter actions (the write surface).

Actions live alongside state on the same object rather than under
a nested

sub-key because the surface is small and
consumers read it via selectors. If the store grows past a dozen
fields, consider splitting into context / actions like the
Zustand v5 "split state and actions" pattern.

Ids of every export format currently registered with the
runtime, in plugin insertion order. Populated from
); tests pass a synthetic list directly.

if nothing is selected yet. Persisted across reloads so the

field

while an export run is in flight. **Never persisted** —
a reload always lands on

so the UI can never be stuck
in a spinner that outlives the request it belonged to.

before any run has
completed. Ephemeral — a reload clears it.
Record the outcome of a run and stamp it with

lifecycle handler (successful
runs) and the export pipeline's catch block (failed runs).

under the hood so the
definition of "initial" lives in exactly one place.

with a new list. Called once
at

A single diagnostic emitted by an exporter.

Warnings are advisory — they do not abort the export. Host apps
typically surface them in a toast or a dev console. Hard failures
should throw

the warning is attached to.

The record is a terse summary (format id, timestamp, ok flag) — no
warning payload, no content blob. UI surfaces that need more

when it threw. An exporter that returns a result with

content.text:
An unsubscribe function. Calling it is idempotent and
detaches the handler immediately.

summary.text:
Release any in-flight debounce timers and forget every live
subscriber so the manager can be garbage-collected cleanly.
calls this from the unmount cleanup that follows

debounce
timer would fire after the host dropped its reference to the
runtime — running plugin hooks against a stale context whose

now throws.

Safe to call multiple times; subsequent calls are no-ops.
The root IR document — the single argument passed to every

(Phase 3). Core
declares the shape; it does not implement the transformation.

Top-level asset manifest. Every asset referenced anywhere in
the tree should appear here exactly once; node-scoped

entries are an optimization for
exporters that process nodes independently.

The root node of the page tree.

Exporters walk this recursively to produce output.

Schema version of this IR document.

Literal
for the initial contract. A future

would be
a breaking change to IR shape and requires a migration shim in

. String (not number) to avoid JSON
floating-point surprises and to match the convention used by
most serialized IR schemas.

An out-of-band resource referenced by the page — images, videos,
fonts, stylesheets, inline scripts, etc.

Assets are listed both at the root of

(so exporters
can emit a manifest or preload block in one pass) and, optionally,
on individual

s (so a node-scoped exporter can
resolve its own dependencies without walking the whole tree).

The same asset may appear in both places — exporters are expected
to deduplicate by

Stable, unique identifier for this asset within the IR.

Typically a hash of the URL or a caller-provided slug. Exporters
use this for deduplication when the same asset is referenced by
multiple nodes.

The asset's category.

Closed union — extending this list is a breaking change to the
IR contract. Use

for anything that doesn't fit the
named categories rather than introducing an ad-hoc string.

Free-form metadata the producer wants to pass through to
consumers (e.g.

for an image).

Exporters should treat unknown keys as forward-compatible — a
missing key must not cause a hard failure.

Absolute or root-relative URL pointing at the asset.

Exporters may rewrite this (e.g. to a CDN or a hashed filename)
during serialization — the IR shape does not constrain the
scheme.

Page-level metadata — title, description, and timestamps the
exporter may surface in

, a feed, or a manifest.

All fields are optional so minimal IR snapshots can be produced
without forcing the caller to synthesize values they don't have.

Timestamps are ISO 8601 strings (not

instances) so the IR
remains JSON-serializable without a custom replacer.
Optional ISO 8601 timestamp of page creation.
Optional ISO 8601 timestamp of most-recent page update.

A single node in the page IR tree.

Structurally recursive: every node may carry a

array of
further nodes, mirroring the component tree the author laid out in
the editor. Leaf nodes omit

entirely rather than setting
it to an empty array (the distinction is not semantically
meaningful, but omitting the key keeps serialized IR snapshots
smaller).

###

is a stable per-node identifier, unique within a
  single

instance. Used by exporters for
  key/anchor/href generation.

are allowed and expected
— exporters deduplicate by
Optional child nodes. Absent on leaf nodes.
Stable per-node identifier, unique within its owning

Serialized prop bag for this node.

Opaque to the IR — exporters are responsible for interpreting

(e.g.

). Exporters dispatch on this value

config, and a childless provider is a bug the type system should
catch at the call site.

Read / write / observe handle passed to every plugin lifecycle call.

### Read access

-

returns a snapshot of the current Puck page data.
  Cheap to call; no subscription is created.
-

is the frozen, merged Studio configuration.

### Write access

- All mutations go through

per architecture §12. Plugins have **no** direct Zustand
  store access — that boundary prevents two plugins from fighting
  over the same store key and ensures every mutation is captured in
  Puck's history stack.

### Observability

-

broadcasts to the plugin event bus so other plugins
  can observe this one (subscribers wire up their own listeners in
— Core does not prescribe a subscription API).

Broadcast an event to the Studio plugin event bus.

Event names are free-form strings — Core does not enforce a
schema. Subscribers validate payloads themselves.

Return a snapshot of the current Puck page data.

Calling this does not subscribe the plugin to future changes —
use the

hook to
react to data updates.

Register a runtime asset resolver for export-time URL rewrites.

Resolvers are consulted by export formats that opt into the
asset-resolution pipeline. A resolver should return

for
URLs it does not own, and a rewritten URL when it does.

Optional Puck config generic so plugins can
receive fully-typed component data. Defaults to the Puck default.
Optional lifecycle method bag returned by a plugin's

.

Every hook is optional. The lifecycle manager (

.

### Ordering

Hooks fire in plugin registration order, which mirrors the order of
the host app's

to abort the
  publish. The host receives the error and can surface it.
- All other hooks' errors are logged and swallowed — a buggy plugin
  must not crash the editor.

### Statelessness

Hooks receive a fresh

pointer at every
call site, so plugins can stay stateless and read the current data
/ config at the moment the hook fires.

Fires after the host app successfully publishes. Use for
post-publish side-effects like cache invalidation or telemetry.
Fires before the host app publishes. May throw

to abort the publish (e.g. validation
failure, missing required fields).

with the fresh data snapshot.

The

argument is passed directly so the plugin does not
need to re-read it from

unmounts. Use for cleanup — subscriptions,
timers, event listeners, intervals, etc.

mounts the Puck editor. Use for one-time setup
(subscribing to external data, priming caches, etc.).

one on

object.
The runtime consumes this block for both diagnostics and

validates this against the installed Core
version. A mismatch throws

at compile time
so host apps fail loud rather than rendering with a misaligned
plugin.

. Used for diagnostics only —
the runtime does not gate behavior on this field.

is optional, so a plugin may:

- Register only hooks (pure lifecycle observer).
- Register only overrides (pure UI contribution).
- Register only export formats (pure serialization plugin).
- Or any combination.

runtime can associate every registered artifact — override,

— two

in

is the forcing function that keeps the two in
sync, which is simpler and equally robust.

other consumer.

Create a lifecycle manager bound to a frozen array of plugin

The registrations are snapshotted at construction time — adding
or removing plugins at runtime would require rebuilding the
manager, which mirrors Puck's mount-time plugin array semantics.

defaults, env vars, and explicit host overrides — in that order.

### Layering

1. **Layer 1 — defaults.**

produces
   the fully-populated default shape. This is the base target for

, so missing keys in higher layers fall back
   to real values rather than

argument
   is treated as an opaque

and layered last, so it
   beats both defaults and env.

After merging, the result is re-parsed through the schema. This
re-parse is load-bearing: it re-runs every validator (URL format,
integer bounds, enum membership) against the merged shape and
catches env-induced type mismatches that layers 1 and 3 would
have passed cleanly on their own.

raised during the final parse is wrapped in a

whose message lists every failing field

shape.

Puck's plugin type is a bag of optional fields (

) — there is no single required field to pin
the check on. This guard looks for **any** of the distinctive Puck
contribution surfaces:

-

— a plugin-provided side panel or toolbar.

An object that contributes none of those is either a vacuous
plugin (which callers should author as a

instead)
or something that was handed to the runtime by mistake. Rejecting
the empty object here means

instead of silently registering a no-op.

Crucially, this guard **also** rejects anything that looks like a
— the two guards are mutually exclusive so

branch.

Pure, synchronous, no side effects.

shape.

The check is intentionally minimal — it validates the three fields
the runtime actually reads during compilation:

1.

is a function.

Everything else on

(name, version,
description) is advisory and not required for the runtime to make
forward progress. A plugin that passes this guard but omits those
fields will still compile; the type system is the forcing function
for completeness at authoring time.

Pure, synchronous, no side effects.

Compose a list of plugin-contributed Puck override slices into a
single

dictionary, with per-key currying so
multiple plugins touching the same key all run.

The input array is consumed in order: earlier entries become
**innermost** wrappers (closer to the default render), later
entries become **outermost**. This matches Puck 0.21's own
internal composition so feeding the result back into

is semantically identical to letting Puck
register each plugin individually — just done once, upfront, so

can also fold in consumer-supplied overrides in the
same pass.

readme.text:
# @anvilkit/core

The Anvilkit Studio runtime — a thin, plugin-driven React shell
around [Puck](https://puckeditor.com/) that lets host apps ship a
visual page editor with export formats, AI generation, and custom
header actions added by installable plugins rather than forked code.

owns the plugin contract, the layered config system,
the lifecycle event bus, the Zustand state slices for Studio-level
concerns (exports, AI, theme), and the

component that
wires all of them to

. Everything above that — component
packages, export plugins, AI plugins — lives in its own

package and is wired in via the plugin API.

## Install
will not ship its own copies. Any

while it resolves the plugin graph, then
mounts
